refinement_sections.content: Our client is a health organization that wants to find out if their new wellness programs help reduce stress among employees. They did a survey but we need to figure out how to analyze the data they collected.

The survey has responses from different departments, but we don't know how stressed employees were before these programs started. We need to look at if stress levels are different between departments.

I think we should use a hypothesis test to see if the wellness programs really make a difference. We might need to use a t-test or ANOVA if there are more than two groups, but I'm not sure which one is best.
We need to think about whether the data is normally distributed and if the variances are the same across the groups. I guess we should check these things before running the test.

I think our null hypothesis could be that the wellness programs have no effect on stress levels, while the alternative hypothesis could be that they do have an effect. We need to outline how we will test these.
After we run the test, we will need to decide if we can reject the null hypothesis or not, which will tell us if the programs are effective.
